title: Wall Street Wants A Fully Controlled DeFi
transcript: Imagine  waking  up  to  find  your  crypto   exchange  account  frozen.  Not  because  of   a  court  order,  but  because  a  government   algorithm  flagged  your  wallet  address  as   suspicious.  It  sounds  like  a  dystopian   central  bank  digital  currency  nightmare.   But  on  the  9th  of  March,  the  US  Treasury   officially  asked  Congress  for  the  legal   authority  to  do  exactly  that.  They  are   requesting  a  statutory  safe  harbor  to   freeze  digital  assets  without  a  warrant,   targeting  a  stable  coin  market  that   currently  holds  over  $262  billion  in   systemic  value.  The  mainstream  media  is   applauding  this  as  a  necessary  crackdown   on  cartel  money  laundering,  but  buried   inside  their  32-page  legislative  request   is  the  architecture  for  the  most   expansive  financial  surveillance  net   since  2001.  My  name  is  Lewis  and  you're   watching  the  Coin  Bureau.   So,  is  this  just  a  necessary  tool  to   fight  illicit  stable  coin  flows?  Are  we   staring  down  the  barrel  of  a  Patriot  Act   2.0  for  Web  3?  Or  is  this  the  final   piece  of  compliance  plumbing  Wall  Street   needs  to  control  decentralized  finance?   That  is  exactly  what  we  are  going  to   cover  today.  First  things  first  though,   let's  look  at  the  catalyst  that  has   privacy  advocates  sounding  the  alarm.  On   the  9th  of  March  2026,  the  US  Treasury   published  a  highly  anticipated  32page   report  detailing  the  evolving  landscape   of  crypto-related  crime.  This  document   was  mandated  under  section  9  of  the   Genius  Act,  the  landmark  stablecoin   legislation  that  President  Trump  signed   into  law  back  in  July  of  2025.  Now,  if   you  read  the  mainstream  financial  press   right  now,  the  headlines  are  entirely   focused  on  one  terrifying  narrative.   They  will  site  reports  from  blockchain   analytics  firms  like  Chalysis  showing   that  Chinese  language  organized  crime   networks  moved  an  estimated  16.1  billion   through  crypto  in  2025.  They  will  point   to  original  Treasury  data  revealing  that   North  Korean  cyber  criminals  stole  at   least  2.8  billion  in  digital  assets  over   the  last  2  years.  and  they  will   highlight  the  $ 1.5  billion  bybit  hack   as  proof  that  the  current  regulatory   perimeter  is  failing.  The  Treasury's   argument  is  that  current  law  enforcement   mechanisms  are  simply  too  slow  to  stop   these  crossber  transfers.  By  the  time   investigators  secure  a  subpoena,  the   stolen  funds  have  already  been  pushed   through  a  mixer,  swapped  into  stable   coins,  and  bridged  across  multiple   networks.  To  the  average  retail  investor   reading  the  news,  this  looks  like  a   standard,  entirely  justified   anti-moneyaundering  initiative.  Nobody   wants  to  see  North  Korea  funding  its   weapons  programs  with  stolen  Bitcoin.   But  if  you  actually  read  the  fine  print   of  the  legislative  recommendations,  a   much  darker  reality  emerges  because  the   Treasury  is  not  just  asking  for  better   enforcement  of  existing  laws.  They  are   asking  Congress  to  fundamentally  rewrite   the  rules  of  financial  privacy  in  the   United  States.  Let's  take  a  closer  look   at  the  exact  figures  the  Treasury  is   using  to  justify  this  power  grab.   According  to  TRM  Labs,  elicit  entities   received  approximately  141  billion  in   stable  coins  in  2025.  That  sounds  like   an  astronomical  figure,  and  it  is,  but   it  represents  less  than  1.2%  of  the   roughly  $4  trillion  in  total  stable  coin   activity  we  saw  last  year.  The   overwhelming  majority  of  stable  coin   volume  is  entirely  lawful,  driven  by   users  seeking  refuge  from  inflation  or   executing  legitimate  crossber  payments.   Yet,  the  Treasury's  proposed  solution   treats  the  entire  ecosystem  as  a   potential  threat  vector.  And  that  brings   us  to  the  specific  legal  mechanisms  that   they  are  demanding.  But  here  is  where   the  story  turns  because  the  real  threat   isn't  the  crackdown  on  cartels.  It  is   the  legal  precedent  being  requested.  The   Treasury  is  urging  Congress  to  enact  a   digital  asset  specific  hold  law.  Put   simply,  this  would  give  financial   institutions  and  crypto  exchanges  a   legal  safe  harbor  to  temporarily  freeze   your  assets  without  a  formal  warrant.   Now,  to  understand  why  this  is  so   revolutionary,  you  have  to  understand   how  the  current  Bank  Secrecy  Act   actually  works.  And  well,  here's  how  it   works.  Under  current  law,  if  a  bank   detects  suspicious  activity,  they're   required  to  file  a  suspicious  activity   report  or  SAR  with  Vincen.  But   crucially,  they're  not  clearly   authorized  to  freeze  your  funds  without   a  court  order,  sanctions  authority,  or   risking  massive  civil  liability.  The  SAR   regime  is  a  disclosure  mechanism,  not  a   freeze  mechanism.  If  an  exchange  freezes   your  account  today  and  they  are  wrong,   well,  you  can  sue  them.  The  proposed   hold  law  completely  removes  that   liability.  It  grants  exchanges  statutory   immunity  to  act  as  a  private  enforcement   arm  of  the  federal  government.  If  their   internal  AI  monitoring  software  flags   your  transaction  as  high  risk,  well,   they  could  lock  your  account   immediately.  No  judge,  no  subpoena,  and   no  due  process  required.  And  this   creates  a  terrifying  legal  paradox.   Public  affairs  attorney  Andrew  Roso   recently  pointed  out  a  massive   structural  flaw  in  this  proposal.  Under   existing  STAR  rules,  platforms  are   legally  prohibited  from  informing  users   that  they  are  under  investigation.  This   is  known  as  the  tipping  off  restriction.   So,  if  an  exchange  freezes  your  assets   under  this  new  hold  law,  well,  they   cannot  legally  tell  you  why.  You  will   wake  up,  find  your  account  locked,  and   be  met  with  a  generic  customer  service   wall  of  silence.  You  will  know  that  you   are  frozen,  but  you  won't  know  why,  and   you  won't  have  a  court-ordered   challenge.  We  have  already  seen  exactly   what  this  looks  like  in  practice.  Cast   your  mind  back  to  February  of  2022   during  the  Canadian  trucker  convoy   protests.  The  Canadian  government   invoked  the  Emergencies  Act  to  freeze   bank  accounts  and  cryptocurrency  wallets   tied  to  the  protesters.  They  bypassed   the  courts  and  ordered  regulated   financial  firms  to  cease  facilitating   transactions  for  dozens  of  targeted   addresses.  A  Canadian  federal  court   later  ruled  that  this  use  of  emergency   powers  was  completely  unconstitutional.   But  the  US  Treasury  is  now  asking   Congress  to  make  the  exact  same  freeze   power  a  permanent  everyday  feature  of   the  American  financial  system.  They   don't  want  to  declare  a  national   emergency.  They  just  want  a  safe  harbor.   And  speaking  of  staying  ahead  of  these   regulatory  shocks  before  they  wipe  out   your  portfolio,  this  is  exactly  why  you   need  the  Coin  Bureau  Telegram  channel  in   your  life.  That  is  where  we  share  the   latest  breaking  news,  provide  deep  dive   alpha,  and  give  you  the  most  important   market  updates.  All  ping  directly  to   your  device  so  that  you  don't  miss  a   thing.  Whether  it's  a  hidden  clause  in  a   Treasury  report  or  massive  onchain  whale   movements,  our  members  see  it  first.  So,   sign  up  today  using  the  link  down  below   or  by  scanning  the  QR  code  on  the   screen.  Now,  back  to  the  Treasury   report.  Because  as  chilling  as  the  hold   law  is  for  centralized  exchanges,  it  is   only  half  of  the  government's  plan.  The   Treasury  knows  that  if  they  squeeze   centralized  exchanges  too  hard,  users   will  simply  migrate  to  decentralized   finance.  And  the  data  shows  that  this   migration  is  already  happening.   According  to  Coin  Gecko,  decentralized   exchanges  saw  their  spot  trading  market   volume  share  double  from  6.9%  in  early   2024  to  nearly  14%  in  2026.  Perpetual   DEX  volume  surged  an  astonishing  176%   last  year  alone.  So  to  capture  this   decentralized  volume,  the  Treasury  has   proposed  what  might  be  the  most   aggressive  expansion  of  financial   surveillance  in  modern  US  history.  They   are  proposing  an  expansion  to  section   311  of  the  USA  Patriot  Act.  Now,  this  is   where  things  get  a  bit  technical,  so   listen  carefully.  Historically,  section   311  grants  the  Treasury  Secretary  the   authority  to  designate  foreign   jurisdictions  or  foreign  banks  as  a   primary  money  laundering  concern.  If  a   foreign  bank  is  designated  under  the   fifth  special  measure,  US  institutions   are  prohibited  from  maintaining   correspondent  accounts  with  them.  It  is   effectively  a  death  sentence  that  cuts   the  target  off  from  the  US  dollar   system.  But  there  is  a  fundamental   constraint  built  into  the  current  law.   Section  311  only  targets  foreign   entities  through  traditional   correspondent  banking  relationships.  The   Treasury  wants  to  blow  that  wall  wide   open.  They  are  asking  Congress  to  create   a  sixth  special  measure.  This  new   special  measure  would  authorize  the   Treasury  to  prohibit  or  impose  strict   conditions  on  specific  digital  asset   transmitts  that  have  absolutely  no   connection  to  a  correspondent  bank.   Let's  be  precise  about  what  is  actually   happening  here.  This  would  allow  the   Treasury  to  designate  specific  smart   contract  addresses,  decentralized   lending  protocols,  or  cross-chain   bridges  as  primary  money  laundering   concerns.  It  completely  bypasses  the   traditional  judicial  process.  We  saw  a   preview  of  this  in  August  of  2022  when   the  Office  of  Foreign  Assets  Control   sanctioned  the  Tornado  Cash  smart   contracts.  That  was  the  first  time  the   government  tried  to  sanction  open-source   code  rather  than  a  person  or  a  corporate   entity.  But  in  November  2024,  the  Fifth   Circuit  Court  of  Appeals  struck  that   down.  The  court  ruled  that  immutable   smart  contracts  cannot  be  owned  or   controlled,  so  they  do  not  meet  the   legal  definition  of  sanctionable   property.  The  government  lost.  OFAC  was   forced  to  officially  lift  the  sanctions   on  tornado  cash  in  March  of  2025.   But  the  Treasury  did  not  give  up.  They   simply  changed  their  strategy.  Because   the  courts  told  them  that  they  lacked   the  statutory  authority  to  sanction   smart  contracts,  they're  now  going   directly  to  Congress  to  get  that  exact   authority  written  into  the  Patriot  Act.   Alex  Thorne,  the  head  of  firmwide   research  at  Galaxy  Digital,  warned  about   this  exact  mechanism  earlier  this  year.   He  stated  that  if  these  powers  become   law,  they  would  represent  the  single   largest  expansion  of  financial   surveillance  authorities  since  the   Patriot  Act  was  originally  passed  in   2001.  This  isn't  just  a  regulatory   tweak.  It  is  a  legislative  kill  switch   for  permissionless  finance.  Now,  to   understand  why  this  is  so  dangerous,  you   need  to  look  at  how  this  integrates  with   Wall  Street's  massive  tokenization  push.   Because  the  mainstream  narrative  will   tell  you  that  traditional  finance  is   finally  embracing  crypto.  Larry  Fink  and   Black  Rockck  are  tokenizing  billions  of   dollars  in  US  treasuries.  The  tokenized   real  world  asset  market  has  exploded   past  $  36  billion.   But  what  Wall  Street  is  quietly  building   is  not  a  bridge  to  decentralized   finance.  It  is  a  compliance  dragnet   designed  to  absorb  crypto  into  the   traditional  financial  surveillance   state.  And  the  technical  plumbing  for   this  is  already  fully  operational.  We   recently  covered  how  institutions  are   building  permissioned  DeFi  using  a   specific  smart  contract  standard  called   ERC  3643.  Unlike  a  standard  ERC20  token,   which  is  permissionless  by  default,  the   ERC  3643  standard  requires  every  single   wallet  to  pass  KYC  and  AML  verification   before  it  could  send  or  receive  any   token.  It  uses  an  onchain  identity   registry  called  onchain  ID  to  map  every   crypto  wallet  to  a  verified  real  world   identity.  But  here  is  the  absolute   kicker.  That  standard  already  has  wallet   level  freezing  and  force  transfer   capabilities  built  directly  into  the   smart  contracts.  It  is  a  mandatory   requirement  of  the  protocol.  The   administrator  of  the  token  can  freeze   your  wallet,  pause  your  transfers,  or   forcefully  claw  back  your  assets  at  any   time.  Togeony,  the  company  behind  this   standard,  now  powers  over  $  32  billion   in  tokenized  assets.  Even  the  DTCC,  the   largest  post-trade  financial   infrastructure  provider  on  the  planet,   has  joined  the  ERC  3643  association.   This  is  becoming  the  default  compliance   layer  for  global  capital  markets,  and  we   are  already  seeing  this  architecture   bleed  into  decentralized  exchanges.  In   February  2026,  Black  Rockck  listed  its   $2.4  $4  billion  biddis  swap.  Retail   investors  cheered  thinking  Wall  Street   was  finally  providing  liquidity  to  DeFi.   But  the  contract  rejects  you   automatically  before  a  single  token   moves  unless  your  wallet  has  been   explicitly  whitelisted  by  Securitize   Black  Rockck's  registered  transfer   agent.  Full  stop.  They're  renting   permissionless  infrastructure  to  run  a   completely  permissioned  gated  financial   product.  Now,  connect  the  dots  between   Wall  Street's  tokenization  standard  and   the  Treasury's  proposed  hold  law.  If   Congress  grants  this  hold  law,  exchanges   and  token  issuers  will  be  legally   shielded  from  civil  liability  when  they   use  those  ERC  3643  freeze  functions.  It   effectively  turns  centralized  exchanges   and  stable  coin  issuers  into  deputized   arms  of  the  federal  government.  If  an   algorithm  flags  your  account,  the   exchange  invokes  the  safe  harbor,   freezes  your  custodial  account,  and   simultaneously  triggers  the  smart   contract  freeze  function  on  your  onchain   assets.  And  if  you  think  the  base  layer   of  Ethereum  is  immune  to  this  pressure,   well,  history  says  otherwise.  In  late   2022,  following  the  initial  tornado   cache  sanctions,  the  percentage  of  OFAC   compliant  blocks  on  the  Ethereum  network   peaked  at  roughly  80%.  Eight  out  of  10   blocks  were  actively  censoring   transactions  to  comply  with  US  Treasury   sanctions.  The  compliance  layer   inevitably  bleeds  into  the  base  layer.   The  censorship  didn't  require  a  protocol   upgrade.  It  only  required  a  handful  of   private  relay  operators  to  make  a  legal   decision  about  which  transactions  they   would  process.  If  the  Treasury  gets  the   power  to  designate  DeFi  protocols  under   the  Patriot  Act,  that  base  layer   censorship  won't  just  be  voluntary.  It   will  be  a  federal  mandate.  So,  what's   the  verdict?  Well,  let's  be  blunt.  The   Treasury's  32-page  report  is  not  just  a   warning.  It  is  a  highly  coordinated   legislative  roadmap.  While  the  stated   goal  of  stopping  2.8  billion  dollars  in   North  Korean  cyber  theft  is  entirely   valid,  the  mechanism  they  are  requesting   fundamentally  breaks  the  premise  of   permissionless  finance.  By  asking  for   the  power  to  freeze  assets  without  a   warrant  and  by  expanding  the  Patriot  Act   to  cover  DeFi  protocols,  the  government   is  building  the  ultimate  compliance   draget.  And  the  market  is  already   pricing  this  dystopian  reality  in.  While   Bitcoin  and  Ethereum  have  struggled  to   reclaim  their  all-time  highs,  privacy   coins  are  seeing  a  massive  resurgence.   Monero  has  surged  over  63%  in  the  past   year,  recently  hitting  a  peak  of  nearly   $800  in  January.  That  is  a  measurable   quantifiable  privacy  premium.  Capital  is   actively  fleeing  transparent  blockchains   in  search  of  censorship  resistance.  The   institutional  adoption  of  crypto  was   always  going  to  come  with  a  regulatory   cost.  We  traded  the  cipher  punk  ethos   for  spot  ETFs  and  Wall  Street  liquidity.   But  this  proposal  proves  that  the   endgame  isn't  just  regulation.  It  is   absolute  control.  The  question  isn't   whether  Wall  Street  and  the  Treasury   will  try  to  build  this  surveillance   architecture.  The  data  proves  that  they   already  are.  The  question  is,  will  the   crypto  community  recognize  the  trap   before  the  doors  lock  from  the  outside?   But  what  do  you  think?  Is  a  statutory   hold  law  a  necessary  evil  to  legitimize   the  crypto  industry  and  stop  illicit   finance?  Or  is  it  a  catastrophic   violation  of  our  fundamental  financial   privacy?  Let  me  know  your  thoughts  in   the  comments  down  below.  And  if  you  want   to  understand  exactly  how  Wall  Street  is   building  KYC  directly  into  smart   contracts,  well,  you  could  check  out  our   video  on  that  right  over  here.  And  if   you  want  to  dive  deeper  into  how   macroeconomic  forces  are  reshaping  the   digital  gold  narrative,  well,  you  could   check  that  video  out  right  over  here.   Thank  you  all  so  much  for  watching  and   I'll  see  you  again  very  soon.  This  is   Lewis  signing  off.
summary_hu: A videó arra figyelmeztet, hogy az Egyesült Államok Pénzügyminisztériuma a törvényhozástól azt kéri, hogy adjon jogi felhatalmazást a digitális eszközök lefoglalására, anélkül hogy bírósági parancs lenne szükséges. A törvényjavaslat különösen a stabilcoin piacra fókuszál, amely több mint 262 milliárd dollárt képvisel, és a kormány a pénzügyi megfigyelés megerősítésére törekszik. A javasolt törvényjavaslat alapja a pénzügyi magánélet radikális átalakítása lenne az Egyesült Államokban, mivel jogi immunitást adna a pénzintézeteknek az ilyen befagyasztásokra. A decentralizált pénzügyi platformokra való átállás egyre növekvő tendenciájára reagálva a Pénzügyminisztérium átfogó pénzügyi megfigyelő rendszer kiterjesztését javasolja, amely bármely digitális eszközt megcélzolhat. A videó szerint a javasolt intézkedések nem csak a pénzmosás ellen irányulnak, hanem alapvetően megkérdőjelezik a szabad pénzügyi rendszer működését.
summary_en: The analysis warns that the U.S. Treasury is requesting Congress to grant legal authority for the freezing of digital assets without the need for a court order. This legislative proposal specifically targets the stablecoin market, which holds over $262 billion, and aims to enhance financial surveillance. The proposed law would fundamentally alter financial privacy rights in the United States, providing legal immunity for financial institutions acting on such freezes. In response to the growing trend of migration to decentralized finance, the Treasury is pushing for an expansive financial surveillance mechanism that could label any digital asset operation as a potential threat. The video argues that these proposed measures are not only aimed at combating money laundering but fundamentally challenge the premise of permissionless finance.
key_points_hu: A Pénzügyminisztérium jogi felhatalmazást kér a digitális eszközök lefoglalására.
A javasolt törvények a pénzügyi megfigyelés drasztikus növelésére irányulnak.
A decentralizált pénzügyekkel kapcsolatos migráció veszélyezteti a szabályozott pénzügyi rendszert.
key_points_en: The Treasury is seeking legal authority to freeze digital assets.
The proposed laws aim to drastically enhance financial surveillance.
The migration to decentralized finance poses a threat to the regulated financial system.
